{{Short description|Psychological phenomenon}}
{{use mdy dates|date=May 2022}}
[[File:Gabriel von Max - Lady Macbeth, 1885.jpg|thumb|A painting by [[Gabriel von Max]] depicting [[Lady Macbeth]] attempting to clean her hand with the folded edge of her dress]]

== Background ==
The '''Lady Macbeth effect''' or '''''Macbeth'' effect''' is a phenomenon in [[psychology]] where a sense of [[Guilt (emotion)|guilt]] or [[Morality|moral]] transgression may increase [[cleaning]] and cleanliness-seeking responses.<ref name="zhong" /> It is named after [[William Shakespeare|Shakespeare]]'s character [[Lady Macbeth]], who obsessively washes herself in an attempt to clear her conscience after orchestrating murder, famously saying, "Out, damned spot!"<ref name=":0">{{Cite news |date=2006-12-10 |title=The Lady Macbeth Effect (Published 2006) |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2006/12/10/magazine/10Section2a.t-9.html |access-date=2025-09-10 |language=en}}</ref> 

This effect of engaging in immoral behaviours can be explained through [[embodied cognition]] -  the idea that actions not only affect the way we think but also shape our sensory-motor systems and how we interact with the environment.

Applied to [[morality]], this means that moral wrongdoing creates psychological discomfort that is physically experienced as a sense of contamination, leading individuals to pursue cleansing behaviours to "wash away" that feeling. For instance, just as someone might instinctively wash their hands after touching something unpleasant, similarly, people who have acted unethically may feel a physical urge to cleanse themselves — even when no literal dirt is involved.

==Key study ==
The Lady Macbeth effect was introduced in 2006 by Chen-Bo Zhong and Katie Liljenquist in the journal ''[[Science (journal)|Science]]''. They conducted the first empirical demonstration of this effect. Given that physical cleansing could function as a surrogate for moral purification, they set out to investigate whether a threat to moral purity activates a need for physical cleansing In their study, participants were asked to recall an ethical or unethical deed from the past, then fill in the missing letters in "W_&nbsp;_H", "SH_&nbsp;_ER" and "S_&nbsp;_P". Those who recalled their immoral deeds were more likely to choose cleanliness-related words like "WASH", "SHOWER" and "SOAP" rather than equally valid alternatives like "WISH", "SHAKER", or "STOP". Their counterparts showed no preference.<ref name="zhong">{{cite journal|last1=Zhong|first1=Chen-Bo|first2=Katie |last2=Liljenquist |title=Washing Away Your Sins: Threatened Morality and Physical Cleansing|journal=Science|year=2006|volume=313|issue=5792|pages=1451–1452|doi=10.1126/science.1130726|pmid=16960010|citeseerx=10.1.1.181.571|url=http://haniff.sg/wp-content/uploads/2013/04/washing-away-your-sins.pdf|bibcode=2006Sci...313.1451Z|s2cid=33103635 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170809003732/http://haniff.sg/wp-content/uploads/2013/04/washing-away-your-sins.pdf|archive-date=2017-08-09}}</ref> 

These findings suggest an implicit association between moral transgression and physical cleanliness. Recalling unethical behaviour makes cleansing-related thoughts more mentally accessible — a finding consistent with embodied cognition, whereby moral discomfort manifests as a felt sense of physical dirtiness.

== Behavioural Consequences ==
In a separate experiment of whether physical washing "cleanses" guilt and moral stains, Zhong and Liljenquist had all participants recount immoral deeds, and found that the group permitted to wash their hands afterwards was much less likely to volunteer without pay for another research study to help a graduate student out compared to the unwashed group, suggesting physical hygiene may have alleviated their moral guilt.<ref name=":0" /> "If cleanliness is related to moral purity," Zhong said, "then the cleanliness of one’s environment could have an impact on moral behaviour."<ref name=":0" />

The above experiment demonstrates the behavioural consequence of the Macbeth effect. Once feelings associated with moral transgression are reduced through cleansing, individuals may feel less compelled to engage in prosocial behaviour. In contrast, those who did not wash their hands appeared more likely to act in ways that could alleviate their prevailing moral discomfort. Additionally, the premise of the volunteerism measure was based on research indicating that a violation of one’s core values drives an individual to restore their “moral self” through compensatory behaviours such as volunteerism.<ref>{{Citation |last=Fiske |first=Alan Page |title=Taboo Trade-Offs: Constitutive Prerequisites for Political and Social Life |date=2000 |work=Political Psychology |pages=47–65 |url=https://doi.org/10.1057/9780230598744_4 |access-date=2026-04-21 |place=London |publisher=Palgrave Macmillan UK |isbn=978-0-333-75104-6 |last2=Tetlock |first2=Philip E.}}</ref>

==Decision-Making Effects ==

In another experiment, experimenters found that engaging in self-cleaning<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Lee|first1=Spike W. S.|first2=Norbert|last2= Schwarz |title=Washing away postdecisional dissonance|journal=Science|year=2010|volume=328|issue=5979|pages=709|doi=10.1126/science.1186799|pmid=20448177|bibcode=2010Sci...328..709L|s2cid=18611420 |url=https://semanticscholar.org/paper/a53673ec17fae0c0cc1e493e6a4b52f7ffbb53bb}}</ref> reduced [[choice-supportive bias]]— the tendency to over-estimate the benefits of a chosen option and under-estimate the benefits of a rejected one. Subjects who engaged in cleaning behaviours no longer felt the need to positively justify the chosen option and could therefore evaluate past decisions more objectively. It may also reduce their tendency to persist with a suboptimal decisions to justify a prior decision. 

==Localisation of the effect==

The effect is apparently localised enough that those who had been asked to lie verbally preferred an oral cleaning product, and those asked to lie in writing preferred a hand cleaning product over the other kind of cleanser and other control items.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Lee|first1=Spike W. S.|first2=Norbert |last2=Schwarz |title=Dirty Hands and Dirty Mouths: Embodiment of the Moral-Purity Metaphor Is Specific to the Motor Modality Involved in Moral Transgression|journal=Psychological Science|year=2010|volume=21|issue=10|pages=1423–1425|doi=10.1177/0956797610382788|pmid=20817782|s2cid=26639040 |url=https://semanticscholar.org/paper/ca41aabc542e1e15ec93bb56f472389f730f1a67}}</ref>

This illustrates how the type of cleansing behaviour an individual pursues is directly tied to the nature of their wrongdoing – much like someone who may wash their mouth after saying something they regret. 

==Individual Differences==
The Lady Macbeth effect is not uniform across all individuals. A study examined whether the Macbeth effect occurs within a sample of 164 undergrad students with high [[Obsessive–compulsive disorder|OCD]] contamination concerns.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Effect of Physical Cleaning on Threatened Morality in Individuals With Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/action/cookieAbsent |access-date=2026-04-21 |website=Sage Journals |language=en |doi=10.1177/2043808718786595|last=Reuven|first=Orna|last2=Liberman|first2=Nira|last3=Dar|first3=Reuven}}</ref> These participants, along with the [[Treatment and control groups|control group]], were allocated to similar experimental conditions and were asked to recall and write about an immoral action from the past to induce feelings of moral discomfort. The individuals were then provided with the opportunity to engage in cleansing behaviours. Subsequently, measures were taken to assess their moral emotions and willingness to volunteer. This design allowed researchers to compare how cleansing influenced guilt and behaviour across individuals with differing levels of contamination sensitivity. 

Results showed that while both groups exhibited the Lady Macbeth effect, it was significantly stronger in individuals with contamination-related OCD symptoms. Moreover, those who cleansed were less likely to engage in prosocial behaviours like volunteering, aligning closely with the primary study's results. Thus, this study demonstrated that the strength of the effect varies across individuals, depending on their sensitivity to feelings of contamination.

These individual differences are not limited to this context — they extend further across different professions. A study investigated how the moral purity link interacts with different professions – engineering and social science students in particular.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Schaefer |first=Michael |date=2019-09-01 |title=Morality and soap in engineers and social scientists: the Macbeth effect interacts with professions |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s00426-017-0937-8 |journal=Psychological Research |language=en |volume=83 |issue=6 |pages=1304–1310 |doi=10.1007/s00426-017-0937-8 |issn=1430-2772|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Participants were exposed to either ethical or unethical scenarios, adapted from Zhong and Liljenquist (2006), to induce moral or immoral states. They then evaluated a range of products, including cleaning items. 

Results showed a significant need for cleansing products in both groups after engaging in the unethical task, highlighting the Lady Macbeth effect. Notably, however, the strength of this effect varied depending on the profession. The effect was much stronger for engineering students compared with social science students. These findings suggest that the strength of the Lady Macbeth effect is influenced by contextual factors, such as professional orientation, further highlighting its variability.

==Critique==
While the Lady Macbeth effect demonstrated notable findings, it failed to replicate within a Spanish sample, raising questions about its universal applicability.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Gámez |first=Elena |last2=Díaz |first2=José M. |last3=Marrero |first3=Hipólito |date=2011 |title=The Uncertain Universality of the Macbeth Effect with a Spanish Sample |url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/spanish-journal-of-psychology/article/abs/uncertain-universality-of-the-macbeth-effect-with-a-spanish-sample/13EE3C31603D4851E03180FE72ED410E |journal=The Spanish Journal of Psychology |language=en |volume=14 |issue=1 |pages=156–162 |doi=10.5209/rev_SJOP.2011.v14.n1.13 |issn=1988-2904|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Specifically, they found no evidence of increased mental accessibility of cleansing-related concepts, no greater desire for cleansing products, nor a greater likelihood of using antiseptic wipes. Furthermore, engaging in cleansing behaviour did not alleviate the sense of physical contamination experienced by participants. Although participants demonstrated sensitivity to moral transgressions and helping behaviour, cleansing did not significantly reduce their threatened moral identity. 

One possible explanation lies in the cross-cultural difference between guilt and shame orientations. Cultures in which moral transgressions are processed more as internal [[Guilt (emotion)|guilt]] (private, self-directed emotion) are susceptible to the physical cleansing response compared to those where wrongdoing is experienced primarily as [[shame]](socially-directed emotion).<ref>{{Cite book |last=Tangney |first=June Price |url=http://archive.org/details/shameguilt0000tang |title=Shame and guilt |date=2002 |publisher=New York: Guilford Press |others=Internet Archive |isbn=978-1-57230-715-5}}</ref> These findings suggest that the Lady Macbeth effect may be less robust than initially proposed and may depend on cultural and contextual factors. 

Additionally, several limitations are evident in the study conducted on individuals with OCD.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Reuven |first=Orna |title=The Effect of Physical Cleaning on Threatened Morality in Individuals With Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/action/cookieAbsent |access-date=2026-04-16 |website=Sage Journals |language=en |doi=10.1177/2167702613485565|last2=Liberman|first2=Nira|last3=Reuven|first3=Dar}}</ref> As the sample primarily consisted of undergraduate students with high contamination-related OCD symptoms, the findings may not be generalisable to a broader population. Furthermore, although the controlled laboratory setting enhances [[internal validity]], it limits the [[ecological validity]] of the results. Similarly, the professions study was limited to engineering and social scientist students, restricting the generalisability of its findings to other occupational groups. The authors also acknowledge inconsistencies in the wider literature, suggesting that the Lady Macbeth effect may not be observed consistently across contexts. Such findings further limit the scope of this effect. 

==Significance ==
The Lady Macbeth effect provides insight into the relationship between the body and moral experience, demonstrating that moral transgressions are not purely cognitive events but are felt physically. While foundational research supports a reliable link between moral guilt and cleansing behaviour, subsequent studies reveal that the effect is shaped by individual, cultural and contextual factors. Replication failures and methodological limitations suggest that the effect may not be as robust as initially proposed. Nevertheless, it remains a significant contribution to our understanding of embodied cognition and the psychology of morality.

==See also==

* [[Ritual purification]]
* [[Self-licensing]]
* [[Replication crisis]]
